perturbation.original_text: "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" means information of either party (whether of a technical, business or other nature) which the other party knows or reasonably should know to be confidential or proprietary information of such party.
perturbation.changed_text: "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" means information of either party (whether of a technical, business or other nature) which the other party *explicitly marks as* confidential or proprietary information of such party.
perturbation.explanation: This change to the definition of 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION' adds a requirement that information must be 'explicitly marked' as confidential. This contradicts Section 8.1, which implies that information a party 'reasonably should know' is confidential also falls under the definition, regardless of explicit marking. This creates ambiguity regarding unmarked information that should reasonably be understood as confidential and whether the receiving party would be liable for its disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_text: SECTION 8. GENERAL PROVISIONS 8.1 CONFIDENTIALITY. During the Term, and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, the receiving party will not disclose to others or use for any purpose of its own, other than in performance of this Agreement, any Confidential Information of the other party. Each party shall take, at a minimum, measures consistent with those taken to protect its own similar types of Confidential Information (and in any event, at least reasonable measures) to protect the other's Confidential Information against disclosures prohibited by this Agreement. Each party acknowledges that its breach of the provisions of this Section 8.1 [CONFIDENTIALITY] will result in immediate and irreparable harm to the other and that money damages alone would be inadequate to compensate such party. Therefore, in the event of a breach of this Section 8.1 [CONFIDENTIALITY] by either party the other party may, in addition to other remedies, immediately obtain and enforce injunctive relief prohibiting the breach or threatened breach or compelling specific performance. Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, the restrictions set forth in this Section 8.1 [CONFIDENTIALITY] shall not apply to any information that: (i) is in or enters the public domain through no fault of the receiving party; (ii) is disclosed to the receiving party by a third party legally entitled to make such disclosure; (iii) is independently developed by the receiving party without reference to any Confidential Information of the other party; or (iv) is required to be disclosed by applicable law, regulation or order of any governmental authority; provided, that in such event, the receiving party shall provide the disclosing party with prior notice that is reasonable in the circumstances of such disclosure and shall use reasonable efforts to cooperate with the disclosing party to minimize the extent and scope of such disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a requirement for explicit marking of confidential information, which directly contradicts Section 8.1. Section 8.1 states that the receiving party will not disclose or use 'any Confidential Information' and must take reasonable measures to protect it. The original definition of 'Confidential Information' included information that a party 'reasonably should know' is confidential. By requiring explicit marking, the changed text excludes information that reasonably should be known to be confidential but is not explicitly marked, creating an in-text contradiction. This contradicts the broader confidentiality obligation in Section 8.1, creating ambiguity and potential unenforceability.

perturbation.original_text: 2.6 EXCLUSIVE SPONSORSHIP. During the Term, Snap will not grant any third party any right to sponsor any products or services in the Exclusive Category on or through the Snap Web Site. For the avoidance of doubt, the parties acknowledge that the foregoing restriction applies only to persistent sponsorship placement as judged by Sponsor at its discretion, and not to run-of-site banner advertisements or other rotating promotional placements.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.6 EXCLUSIVE SPONSORSHIP. During the Term, Snap will not grant any third party any right to sponsor any products or services in the Exclusive Category on or through the Snap Web Site. For the avoidance of doubt, the parties acknowledge that the foregoing restriction applies only to persistent sponsorship placement as judged by *Snap* at its discretion, and not to run-of-site banner advertisements or other rotating promotional placements.
perturbation.explanation: The change in section 2.6 shifts the discretion of judging persistent sponsorship placement to Snap instead of the Sponsor. This directly contradicts the definition in Exhibit A, Paragraph 1, where the 'Exclusive Category is PROVIDERS OF TRAVEL SERVICE,' which gives Sponsor exclusivity and implied discretion. This creates uncertainty as Snap could approve sponsorships that Sponsor deems competitive.
perturbation.contradicted_text: EXHIBIT A PAYMENTS AND EXCLUSIVITY 1. The Exclusive Category is "PROVIDERS OF TRAVEL SERVICE". 2. The up-front payment called for by Section 3.1 will be [*]. 3. Sponsor shall pay the following non-accrued obligation quarterly fees on or prior to the following dates: June 30, 1999 [*] September 30, 1999 [*] December 31, 1999 [*] March 31, 2000 [*] June 30, 2000 [*] September 30, 2000 [*] December 31, 2000 [*]
perturbation.justification: The modification shifts the discretion of judging 'persistent sponsorship placement' from the Sponsor to Snap. Given that Exhibit A defines the 'Exclusive Category' as 'PROVIDERS OF TRAVEL SERVICE,' this directly contradicts the Sponsor's implied exclusivity rights by allowing Snap to potentially approve competing sponsorships, creating a clear conflict and uncertainty about enforcement. This aligns with the Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 TERMINATION. Either party may terminate the Term upon not less than sixty (60) days prior written notice to the other party of any breach hereof by such other party. In addition, either party may terminate the Term immediately upon written notice in the event that the other party shall fail to do business in the normal course or become subject to any bankruptcy, assignment for creditors, corporate dissolution or similar proceeding.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 TERMINATION. Either party may terminate the Term upon not less than sixty (60) days prior written notice to the other party of any *minor* breach hereof by such other party. In addition, either party may terminate the Term immediately upon written notice in the event that the other party shall fail to do business in the normal course or become subject to any bankruptcy, assignment for creditors, corporate dissolution or similar proceeding.
perturbation.explanation: This change in section 4.2 modifies the termination clause by inserting the word 'minor.' This creates a contradiction because the agreement does not define what constitutes a 'minor' breach, and the original text allows termination for *any* breach. Section 5.1(c) contains a warranty that the use of the Snap Content will not infringe any Intellectual Property Right or other right of any third party. A minor breach could theoretically be the infringement of a right of a third party. Making the use of the word minor, creates a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5.1 SNAP REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES. Snap represents and warrants to Sponsor that: (a) it has full power and authority to enter into this Agreement; (b) the execution, delivery and performance by Snap of this Agreement will not violate any law, statute or other governmental regulation, and will not violate any other agreement or instrument to which Snap is a party; and (c) the use of the Snap Content will not violate or infringe any Intellectual Property Right or other right of any third party.
perturbation.justification: The modification to section 4.2 introduces the term 'minor breach,' which is undefined, creating ambiguity. The original text allowed termination for *any* breach. This contradicts section 5.1(c), which warrants that the use of the Snap Content will not infringe any Intellectual Property Right or other right of any third party. A breach of this warranty, even a 'minor' one, could still lead to termination under the original clause, but the modified clause introduces uncertainty as to whether such a 'minor' breach warrants termination, leading to in-text contradiction because the definition of minor is undefined.
